request.question: What company published a fantasy tabletop role-playing game originally designed by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson that has a book of rules that lack the complete set of rules for the game, and only includes rules for use by players of the game?

title: Player's Handbook
Blackmoor (supplement)
Dungeons & Dragons (1974)
Dungeons & Dragons Basic Set
Dungeons & Dragons
Greyhawk (supplement)
Paranoia (role-playing game)
Gary Gygax
Don't Give Up the Ship!
Greyhawk
sentences: The Player's Handbook (Players Handbook in first edition "Advanced Dungeons & Dragons" ("AD&D")) is a book of rules for the fantasy role-playing game "Dungeons & Dragons" ("D&D").
It does not contain the complete set of rules for the game, and only includes rules for use by players of the game.
Additional rules, for use by Dungeon Masters (DMs), who referee the game, can be found in the "Dungeon Master's Guide".
Many optional rules, such as those governing extremely high-level players, and some of the more obscure spells, are found in other sources.

Blackmoor is a supplementary rulebook (product designation TSR 2004) of the original edition of the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game written by Dave Arneson (with a foreword by Gary Gygax).
The original Dungeons & Dragons (commonly abbreviated "D&D") boxed set by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson was published by TSR, Inc. in 1974.
It initially included the original edition of the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game.
Its product designation was TSR 2002.
The Dungeons & Dragons Basic Set is a set of rulebooks for the "Dungeons & Dragons" ("D&D") fantasy role-playing game.
First published in 1977, it saw a handful of revisions and reprintings.
The first edition was written by J. Eric Holmes based on Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson's original work.
Later editions were edited by Tom Moldvay, Dave Cook, and Frank Mentzer.

Dungeons & Dragons (abbreviated as D&D or DnD) is a fantasy tabletop role-playing game (RPG) originally designed by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson, and first published in 1974 by Tactical Studies Rules, Inc. (TSR).
The game has been published by Wizards of the Coast (now a subsidiary of Hasbro) since 1997.
It was derived from miniature wargames with a variation of the "Chainmail" game serving as the initial rule system.
"D&D"'s publication is commonly recognized as the beginning of modern role-playing games and the role-playing game industry.
Greyhawk is a supplementary rulebook written by Gary Gygax and Robert J. Kuntz for the original edition of the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game.
It has been called "the first and most important supplement" to the original "D&D" rules.
By adding a combat system, it severed all ties with "Chainmail", making "D&D" a truly stand-alone game system.

Although the name of the book was taken from the home campaign supervised by Gygax and Kuntz based on Gygax's imagined Castle Greyhawk and the lands surrounding it, "Greyhawk" did not give any details of the castle or the campaign world; instead, it explained the rules that Gygax and Kuntz used in their home campaign, and introduced a number of character classes, spells, concepts and monsters used in all subsequent editions of "D&D".
Paranoia is a dystopian science-fiction tabletop role-playing game originally designed and written by Greg Costikyan, Dan Gelber, and Eric Goldberg, and first published in 1984 by West End Games.
Since 2004 the game has been published under license by Mongoose Publishing.
The game won the Origins Award for Best Roleplaying Rules of 1984 and was inducted into the Origins Awards Hall of Fame in 2007.

"Paranoia" is notable among tabletop games for being more competitive than co-operative, with players encouraged to betray one another for their own interests, as well as for keeping a light-hearted, tongue in cheek tone despite its dystopian setting.

Ernest Gary Gygax ( ) (July 27, 1938 – March 4, 2008) was an American game designer and author best known for co-creating the pioneering role-playing game "Dungeons & Dragons" ("D&D") with Dave Arneson.
Gygax has been described as the father of "D&D".
Don't Give Up the Ship is a set of rules for conducting Napoleonic era naval wargames.
The game was published by Guidon Games in 1972 and republished by TSR, Inc. in 1975.
It was the first collaboration between Dave Arneson and Gary Gygax, the co-creators of "Dungeons & Dragons".
"D&D" fans may also recognize the name of contributor Mike Carr, who edited the rules and researched the historical single ship actions that are included as game scenarios.
Greyhawk, also known as the World of Greyhawk, is a fictional world designed as a campaign setting for the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy roleplaying game.

Although not the first campaign world developed for "Dungeons & Dragons"—Dave Arneson's "Blackmoor" campaign predated it by over a year — the world of Greyhawk was the setting most closely identified with the development of the game from 1972 until 2008.

The world itself started as a simple dungeon under a castle designed by Gary Gygax for the amusement of his children and friends, but it rapidly expanded to include not only a complex multi-layered dungeon environment, but also the nearby city of Greyhawk, and eventually an entire world.

In addition to the campaign world, which was published in several editions over twenty years, Greyhawk was also used as the setting for many adventures published in support of the game, as well as for RPGA's massively shared "Living Greyhawk" campaign from 2000–2008.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: What company published a fantasy tabletop role-playing game originally designed by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson that has a book of rules that lack the complete set of rules for the game, and only includes rules for use by players of the game?
hop1_docs: Dungeons & Dragons | Dungeons & Dragons (abbreviated as D&D or DnD) is a fantasy tabletop role-playing game (RPG) originally designed by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson, and first published in 1974 by Tactical Studies Rules, Inc. (TSR).  The game has been published by Wizards of the Coast (now a subsidiary of Hasbro) since 1997.  It was derived from miniature wargames with a variation of the "Chainmail" game serving as the initial rule system.  "D&D"'s publication is commonly recognized as the beginning of modern role-playing games and the role-playing game industry.

Dungeons & Dragons (1974) | The original Dungeons & Dragons (commonly abbreviated "D&D") boxed set by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson was published by TSR, Inc. in 1974.  It initially included the original edition of the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game.  Its product designation was TSR 2002.

Player's Handbook | The Player's Handbook (Players Handbook in first edition "Advanced Dungeons & Dragons" ("AD&D")) is a book of rules for the fantasy role-playing game "Dungeons & Dragons" ("D&D").  It does not contain the complete set of rules for the game, and only includes rules for use by players of the game.  Additional rules, for use by Dungeon Masters (DMs), who referee the game, can be found in the "Dungeon Master's Guide".  Many optional rules, such as those governing extremely high-level players, and some of the more obscure spells, are found in other sources.

Don't Give Up the Ship! | Don't Give Up the Ship is a set of rules for conducting Napoleonic era naval wargames.  The game was published by Guidon Games in 1972 and republished by TSR, Inc. in 1975.  It was the first collaboration between Dave Arneson and Gary Gygax, the co-creators of "Dungeons & Dragons".  "D&D" fans may also recognize the name of contributor Mike Carr, who edited the rules and researched the historical single ship actions that are included as game scenarios.

Gary Gygax | Ernest Gary Gygax ( ) (July 27, 1938 – March 4, 2008) was an American game designer and author best known for co-creating the pioneering role-playing game "Dungeons & Dragons" ("D&D") with Dave Arneson.  Gygax has been described as the father of "D&D".

Blackmoor (supplement) | Blackmoor is a supplementary rulebook (product designation TSR 2004) of the original edition of the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game written by Dave Arneson (with a foreword by Gary Gygax).

Dungeons & Dragons Basic Set | The Dungeons & Dragons Basic Set is a set of rulebooks for the "Dungeons & Dragons" ("D&D") fantasy role-playing game.  First published in 1977, it saw a handful of revisions and reprintings.  The first edition was written by J. Eric Holmes based on Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson's original work.  Later editions were edited by Tom Moldvay, Dave Cook, and Frank Mentzer.
response.summary_1: Tactical Studies Rules, Inc. (TSR) — the original publisher of Dungeons & Dragons, the game whose Player's Handbook "does not contain the complete set of rules" and "only includes rules for use by players." (passages 1, 3)
response.hop2_query: Tactical Studies Rules Dungeons & Dragons Player's Handbook "does not contain the complete set of rules"
hop2_docs: Player's Handbook | The Player's Handbook (Players Handbook in first edition "Advanced Dungeons & Dragons" ("AD&D")) is a book of rules for the fantasy role-playing game "Dungeons & Dragons" ("D&D").  It does not contain the complete set of rules for the game, and only includes rules for use by players of the game.  Additional rules, for use by Dungeon Masters (DMs), who referee the game, can be found in the "Dungeon Master's Guide".  Many optional rules, such as those governing extremely high-level players, and some of the more obscure spells, are found in other sources.

Player's Handbook II | Player's Handbook II is the title of a third edition "Dungeons & Dragons" supplement.  It is a handbook of rules and guidelines for the "Dungeons & Dragons" role-playing game.  As the name implies, the book is a supplement to the edition's "Player's Handbook".  It introduces supplemental rules, new spells and new classes.

Dungeons & Dragons Tactics | Dungeons & Dragons Tactics is a tactical role-playing game released on the PlayStation Portable handheld video game console.  It is set in the world of "Dungeons & Dragons" and uses a strict interpretation of the "Dungeons & Dragons 3.5 Edition" rule set.

Arcana Unearthed | Arcana Unearthed (properly Monte Cook's Arcana Unearthed, ISBN  ) is a role-playing game created by Monte Cook and first published in 2003.  Described as a "variant player's handbook", the 256-page hardcover core rulebook bears many similarities to the "Players Handbook" of 3rd Edition "Dungeons & Dragons", on which Cook worked a few years prior.  In terms of rules, "Arcana Unearthed" is based on the d20 system of "Dungeons & Dragons", although due to including rules for character advancement it cannot be an official d20 system product.  (It does, however, use the Open Gaming License.)

The Official RPGA Tournament Handbook | The Official RPGA Handbook is an adventure module for the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game, set in the game's World of Greyhawk campaign setting.  TSR, Inc. published the module in 1987 for the first edition "Advanced Dungeons & Dragons" rules.  It is part of the "C" series of modules, a set of unrelated adventures originally designed for competition play ("c" being the first letter in "competition").

Epic Level Handbook | The Epic Level Handbook is a rule-book by Wizards of the Coast for the 3rd edition of "Dungeons & Dragons".  The book was published in 2001, and contains optional game rules for playing characters who have reached a higher experience level than is covered in the standard rules.  This is referred to in the book as "epic level" play.

Cavaliers and Roundheads | Cavaliers and Roundheads is a set of rules for English Civil War miniature wargaming.  It was written by Gary Gygax and Jeff Perren and published by Tactical Studies Rules (later TSR, Inc.) in 1973.  The unassuming booklet was the first product released by the company better known for "Dungeons and Dragons".
response.summary_2: Tactical Studies Rules (TSR). TSR was the company better known for publishing Dungeons & Dragons, whose Player's Handbook "does not contain the complete set of rules" and includes rules intended for players.
